chapters.title: When milliseconds became money on Wall Street
chapters.text:
Exchange platforms advertise fast execution, yet the speeds they deliver still fall short of what physics permits on a straight fiber path between major trading hubs.

Light through glass can shuttle an order from Chicago to New York and return in about twelve milliseconds — the theoretical floor for that corridor.

Measured reality often runs slower. Engineers once logged round trips as long as sixteen milliseconds. The fastest commercially available route — traders called it the Gold Route — clocked in at 14.65 milliseconds. Control that line and you could harvest price differences between the two cities before slower participants even saw the move.

Telecom bosses frequently failed to understand why one millisecond mattered. Verizon, for instance, treated connection speed as a routine service metric, not a financial weapon. Yet their path beat rivals by roughly two milliseconds, which translated into enormous value on Wall Street. The gap came from routing: data snaked along highways and rail rights-of-way instead of following the shortest possible wired corridor.

Dan Spivey, a trader at the Chicago Board Options Exchange, decided to build the direct route himself. He secured permits and capital for Spread Networks. His surprise came later: many finance professionals still dismissed millisecond edges and refused to purchase access. The business model depended on scarcity — faster lines only commanded premium prices while alternatives remained scarce.

By 2010 construction continued with zero signed customers. Spread Networks operated in secrecy to protect pricing power and deter imitators. Only three months before launch did they approach the highest-compensated traders in finance. Reactions ranged from curiosity to outright disbelief that such tiny intervals could matter.

What follows traces how coding talent and network latency became decisive advantages, how carriers grew rich serving traders, what velocity did to market integrity, and how privileged information distorted the natural rhythm of buying and selling.

chapters.title: Why stale market data can cost you everything
chapters.text:
Brad Katsuyama represented Royal Bank of Canada — ranked among the world's largest banks yet a lightweight on Wall Street. He transferred from Toronto to New York in 2002 and quickly earned regard as a top performer inside RBC.

Friction arrived late in 2006 after RBC acquired Carlin Financial, a U.S. electronic trading shop, for $100 million. Katsuyama inherited Carlin's systems. One afternoon his monitor displayed 10,000 Intel shares available at $22. He moved to buy — and the quote evaporated before the order filled.

Screens suggest stability, but displayed prices are snapshots that reshuffle every millisecond.

Tech support insisted the problem sat between keyboard and chair. Katsuyama invited engineers to observe live trading. Each time he clicked, liquidity vanished and prices jumped, leaving the room without answers.

Someone out there was using the fact that stock market orders arrived at different times at different exchanges to front-run orders from one market to another. ~ Michael Lewis

The pattern pointed to front-running: an unseen counterparty detected his intent, purchased shares on other venues first, and resold at a higher price. High-frequency trading desks were the prime suspects.

Investigators eventually blamed distance — Katsuyama's desk sat in Manhattan while matching engines operated across the river in New Jersey, delaying his feed. They also emphasized market crowding: hundreds or thousands of actors trading concurrently. In that environment, whoever received information first held a structural edge.

Did you know? Wall Street remains the world's largest stock exchange. The Wall Street Journal reported average daily trading near $467 billion in single-stock options during 2021.

chapters.title: How stock markets traded skill for speed
chapters.text:
Ronan Ryan emigrated from Ireland in 1990. His parents stretched resources to send him through a private school in Greenwich, which opened a door to MCI Communications in New York. There he studied how packets actually move — rarely in straight lines, but hopping from carrier to carrier along indirect paths. A voice call from New York to Florida might traverse multiple switches and regions before arriving.

The U.S. stock market shifted from having trading skills to having trading speeds that could execute trades in nanoseconds.

Public imagination pictures a direct wire between cities. Real circuits weave through partners like Verizon, BellSouth, and MCI. The elapsed time is latency, determined by three components: boxes (hardware housed in carrier facilities), logic (software governing those boxes), and lines (fiber strands linking sites).

The U.S. financial markets had always been either corrupt or about to be corrupted. ~ Michael Lewis

Physical fiber length dominated latency calculations. Mastering that math made Ryan a prized consultant — every desk wanted instructions on shaving microseconds off execution.

Wall Street had long rewarded relationships and intuition; now it rewarded whoever could move bits across the country a fraction faster than the next desk. Ryan's immigrant work ethic and telecom fluency placed him at the center of that shift.

Katsuyama hired him to head high-frequency strategy inside RBC. Their bargain was reciprocal tutoring: Katsuyama would explain market structure while Ryan decoded the technology underneath.

Ryan later discovered that finance firms would pay premiums that dwarfed traditional telecom contracts — a revelation that redirected his entire career trajectory toward Wall Street.

chapters.title: The programmer who embodied high-frequency trading
chapters.text:
Sergey Aleynikov wrote code capable of pushing vast datasets through pipelines at extraordinary velocity. Recruiters pulled him onto Wall Street during a regulatory turning point — fragmentation accelerated under Regulation National Market System (Reg NMS), the federal framework meant to strengthen the National Market System and modernize equity trading.

Goldman Sachs handed him three overlapping mandates. First, merge feeds from thirteen public exchanges into a single unified stream traders could read as one market.

In the market, decisions are now solely made by computers, removing human responsibility.

Second, stand up a private ticker plant that surfaced actionable opportunities and refactor legacy software before rival firms upgraded theirs.

Third, engineer order-entry tools that could fire transactions automatically. Aleynikov did not yet recognize that he was effectively assembling an in-house high-frequency trading stack.

Each assignment intertwined with the others — faster data ingestion fed smarter algorithms, which in turn demanded faster execution rails. The workload revealed how deeply Goldman depended on code that Aleynikov barely had time to understand before the next upgrade cycle began.

Months of grinding work led to a blunt recommendation: true high-frequency capability required abandoning the old platform entirely and rebuilding from the ground up. Executives balked, prioritizing quarterly targets while competitors invested in ever more advanced infrastructure.

chapters.title: Loyalty rarely survives on Wall Street
chapters.text:
Peers considered Aleynikov among the finest programmers of his generation. Competing high-frequency shops courted him aggressively; his contributions influenced how multiple firms performed. During the 2008 crisis a rival extended an offer, yet he wished to remain at Goldman Sachs. Management matched compensation and he stayed.

A subsequent proposal won him over — not through salary alone, but because it promised a greenfield trading platform built without legacy constraints. Compensation reached roughly one million dollars annually, coupled with a posting nearer his family.

He resigned, boarded a flight to Chicago, and declined a richer counteroffer from former bosses. Several coworkers asked to leave with him; senior leaders pleaded for him to reconsider and raised the financial package again. None of it outweighed the appeal of designing systems without inherited constraints.

At the gate, three men in dark clothing identified themselves as federal agents. He was arrested immediately on allegations of misappropriating Goldman Sachs proprietary code.

That evening he told his wife to permit an FBI search of their residence despite the absence of a warrant. At trial, prosecutors framed his statements to suggest calculated evasiveness.

The court imposed an eight-year federal sentence without parole.

Exercise caution and seek legal counsel before consenting to searches or making statements to law enforcement.

The case became a cautionary tale across Silicon Valley and finance: talent is portable, but intellectual property boundaries are aggressively enforced when billions hang in the balance.

chapters.title: Building a fairer corner of the market
chapters.text:
Around 2008 Katsuyama concluded the equity market had become a different species — technically sophisticated firms harvested latency imbalances while ordinary participants paid the price. He started recruiting young engineers who could think in microseconds.

Rob Park joined first and helped screen later hires. Dan Aisen emerged from a pile of RBC resumes; teammates nicknamed him the puzzle master for his analytical gifts. Katsuyama requested more minds like his, and Aisen brought in Francis Chung to round out the unit.

The group's ambition was defensive, not predatory: construct a trading environment where retail and institutional investors could transact without feeding high-frequency predators.

The puzzle masters catalogued three abuses driving unfair outcomes. The first was front-running — high-frequency traders anticipating and jumping ahead of incoming orders.

Front running happens when high-frequency traders predict and act on your trades ahead of you.

Second came rebate arbitrage, where firms collected exchange incentives without providing genuine liquidity. Third was slow-market arbitrage — observing a price update on one server and trading on another before slower connections caught up.

Each tactic depended on superior speed.

Katsuyama's team spent months modeling how predatory flows exploited tiny timing gaps. They concluded that fairness required architecture, not just rules — a physical design that denied speed merchants their favorite weapons.

Their answer was the Investors Exchange (IEX). By coiling fiber so every broker sat the same effective distance from the matching engine, they stripped away most advantages gained purely from proximity or faster lines. The goal was a protected pool where investors could interact without subsidizing firms that existed mainly to exploit delays.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Public understanding of the stock market still resembles the pre-digital era, partly because modern mechanics are genuinely difficult to grasp. Algorithms, speed games, and cutthroat competition have replaced much of the old culture, and corporate loyalty between trading houses has largely disappeared. Pieced together, these stories portray post-crisis Wall Street in unfamiliar colors.

Velocity now outweighs traditional market knowledge as the decisive edge. Carriers who trim milliseconds from data paths collect outsized fees from financial clients eager for any advantage.

Firms remain secretive about internal practices. Automated strategies execute at scales and speeds no human trader could replicate. Anyone entering the field today must budget heavily for engineering talent alongside market research. Artificial intelligence and quantum computing increasingly function as levers the largest players use to influence prices.

Forecasting the market's shape twenty years hence is a fool's errand — especially given how poorly experts predicted the last transformation. Technology will keep accelerating change, and hindsight will likely reveal a far more elaborate ecosystem than we imagine now. Until then, understanding how speed reshaped incentives remains essential for anyone who trades, invests, or simply wonders where their retirement savings travel each millisecond.

Try this



• If you're new to investing, pay attention to the internet speed available where you trade.
• Steer clear of high-frequency strategies as a beginner.
• Consider the Investors Exchange (IEX) as an alternative built to reduce speed-based exploitation.

Read it till the end?
